coredata.dc:title: Unlocking multifaceted applications of fava bean empty pods-derived CQDs for smart multi-target nanoprobing of pH, temperature, and p-nitrophenol

coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained enormous interest as a dominant class of sensors with multiple applications owing to their unique optical features. In this study, we valorized fava bean empty pods (FBEP) as a costless plant waste for the synthesis of unique multifunctional FBEP-CQDs via a 5-min microwave treatment. The synthesized FBEP-CQDs show superlative thermosensitive, pH-responsive, and nanosensing abilities. The FBEP-CQDs were used as an accurate nanothermometer over the temperature range of 30–70 °C. Moreover, the FBEP-CQDs showed excellent performance as a pH sensor, and they have been favorably applied for pH estimation of soft drinks implying the potential feasible application of the designed pH nanosensors. Based on the selective response of FBEP-CQDs toward p-nitrophenol (PNP), we constructed a fluorescence “on–off” sensor for its quantification in sea, river, and tap water within the concentration range of 7.2–360.0 μM with a detection limit of 2.31 μM. The proposed probe demonstrated excellent stability, selectivity, and greenness. This is attributed to the exclusion of hazardous chemicals, the use of renewable and inexpensive plant waste as a feedstock, and the utility of a low-energy and low-cost microwave-assisted synthesis. The developed approach merits multifunctionality, feasible implementation, cost-efficiency, sustainability, and applicability in low-income laboratories. This strategy offers a promising FBEP-CQDs-based multi-target platform for environmental contaminant, pH, and temperature sensing while supporting the circular economy practice.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphical illustration of the synthesis process of FBEP-CQDs from empty fava bean pods by microwave irradiation at 1700 W for 5 min, followed by sonication, centrifugation, and filtration, showing the multiple applications of the FBEP-CQDs.

(a) Effect of microwave treatment time on the QY of the synthesized CQDs and (b) fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs synthesized from fava beans empty pods harvested in different months (May, August, and September). Spectra and measurements were done in distilled water at room temperature (25 ο C).

(a) TEM image, (b) a histogram of the particle size distribution, (c) XRD-diffraction pattern, (d) FT-IR, (e) Zeta potential distribution curve, and XPS survey scan spectrum of the synthesized FBEP-CQDs.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs in the presence of different concentrations of PNP (7.2–360 µM) and the inset shows the corresponding calibration curve and PNP structure, (b) %E of the observed and corrected fluorescence of N-CQDs after addition of different concentrations of PNP, (c) the Stern-Volmer's curves of N-CQDs-PNP system at different temperatures, and (d) mechanism of dynamic quenching of FBBP-derived CQDs fluorescence.

(a) Relationship between fluorescence intensity of FBBP-CQDs and pH, (b) fluorescence spectra of FBBP-CQDs over pH 3.0–6.0 and the inset is the corresponding calibration curve, (c) fluorescence spectra of FBBP-CQDs over pH 8.0–12.0 and the inset is the corresponding calibration curve, and (d) temperature dependence of the FBBP-CQDs fluorescence emission and the inset is the corresponding calibration curve.
Average of relative viability of WI-38 normal cell lines (%) versus different FBEP-CQDs concentrations (µg/mL).
Results of using various temperature settings to calculate the quenching constants of FBEP-CQDs-PNP system using Stern-Volmer analysis.
Intra-day and inter-day accuracy and precision data for the assay of PNP by the proposed spectrofluorimetric method.

head.ce:title: Unlocking multifaceted applications of fava bean empty pods-derived CQDs for smart multi-target nanoprobing of pH, temperature, and p-nitrophenol

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained enormous interest as a dominant class of sensors with multiple applications owing to their unique optical features. In this study, we valorized fava bean empty pods (FBEP) as a costless plant waste for the synthesis of unique multifunctional FBEP-CQDs via a 5-min microwave treatment. The synthesized FBEP-CQDs show superlative thermosensitive, pH-responsive, and nanosensing abilities. The FBEP-CQDs were used as an accurate nanothermometer over the temperature range of 30–70 °C. Moreover, the FBEP-CQDs showed excellent performance as a pH sensor, and they have been favorably applied for pH estimation of soft drinks implying the potential feasible application of the designed pH nanosensors. Based on the selective response of FBEP-CQDs toward p-nitrophenol (PNP), we constructed a fluorescence “on–off” sensor for its quantification in sea, river, and tap water within the concentration range of 7.2–360.0 μM with a detection limit of 2.31 μM. The proposed probe demonstrated excellent stability, selectivity, and greenness. This is attributed to the exclusion of hazardous chemicals, the use of renewable and inexpensive plant waste as a feedstock, and the utility of a low-energy and low-cost microwave-assisted synthesis. The developed approach merits multifunctionality, feasible implementation, cost-efficiency, sustainability, and applicability in low-income laboratories. This strategy offers a promising FBEP-CQDs-based multi-target platform for environmental contaminant, pH, and temperature sensing while supporting the circular economy practice.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Since their first synthesis, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) acquired a tremendous importance in various applications such as electronics, light emitting diodes, biomedicine, drug delivery, and sensing [1,2]. The wide scale of applications of CQDs is attributable to their exceptional features such as large surface area, extremely small size, photostability, good photo luminous activity, catalytic properties, and low toxicity. Besides, the properties of CQDs can be regulated by selection of feedstock material and synthesis route as well as heteroatom doping that can control chemical characteristics of CQDs [3]. The current synthesis methods of CQDs include chemical oxidation, thermal pyrolysis, and hydrothermal methods. Nevertheless, these synthesis procedures require specific instruments, chemicals and organic solvents, and necessitate several steps and long heating times at extremely high temperatures. Thus, researchers have done major research to adopt environmentally benign synthetic paths for manufacturing CQDs. Microwave-driven synthesis is considered a very effective route for energy and time efficient cleaner fabrication of CQDs [3]. Meanwhile, significant research has also been done on utilizing natural biomass for synthesizing CQDs while excluding chemicals and solvents [4–10]. This approach enhances the environmental friendliness and minimizes the harmful impact on the ecosystem.

Herein, we report a straightforward synthesis of CQDs via a single-step microwave irradiation of fava bean empty pods. Fava bean (Vicia faba L.) is a widely cultivated legume crop for human and animal consumption. The edible part of fava bean is the seeds, while empty pods remain as discarded waste and compose about 70–75 % (w/w) of the whole pod yield. Inappropriately, most of this waste is frequently disposed of by burning or dumping it in landfills, polluting the air and water. The empty pods of fava beans contain considerable quantities of bioactive phytochemicals and minerals [11] which encouraged us to utilize them as a raw material for manufacturing CQDs. This approach supports the sustainability goals and corroborates the practices of circular economy by reusing and recycling wastes, decreasing material consumption, and diminishing waste generation.

The prepared fava bean empty pods-derived CQDs (FBEP-CQDs) showed excellent fluorescence and unique properties that enriched their scale of applications. First, the prepared FBEP-CQDs were utilized as a sensitive turn-off fluorescence nanosensor for detection of p-nitrophenol (PNP) in environmental water samples. PNP is a chemical precursor widely used in the manufacture of some drugs, explosives, insecticides, and synthetic colors. These multiple uses allow PNP to enter the environment through several waste streams causing diverse hazards due to its significant stability and resistance to chemical/biological degradation, toxicity, abundant global production, and frequent occurrence in water [12]. Thus, PNP has been listed as a priority water pollutant by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency [13]. Monitoring of PNP in aquatic environment is mandatory. For this aim, nanomaterial-based methods have been applied for the detection of PNP in environmental water [14–21]. However, most of these techniques depend on nanomaterials synthesized from chemical substrates via multi-steps synthetic routes that need high temperatures for long times. Additionally, the most utilized nanomaterials for PNP detection are CQDs conjugated with metals or silicone nanoparticles [14–18,20,21]. These latter types of nanomaterials exert toxic threats to the human body and the environment [22]. Consequently, harnessing the new green FBEP-CQDs as a probe for sensing and detecting PNP in different environmental waters is important and valuable.

Besides, our investigations revealed the pH-sensitive fluorescence of the prepared FBEP-CQDs as an intriguing characteristic promising for application as a pH-nanoprobe. The fluorescence spectra of the prepared FBEP-CQDs changed as the pH changed, where the increase of pH over the range of 3.0–6.0 resulted in a gradual increase of the fluorescence intensity. A further increase of the pH over the range of 8.0–12.0 is associated with red shift of the fluorescence spectra and a gradual increase of the fluorescence. This characteristic permits the use of the prepared FBEP-CQDs as a pH nanosensor. It is well known that pH measurement is important for different fields like environmental, industrial, and biomedical sectors. However, the traditional optical and fluorescence pH-sensors based on organic dyes have significant limitations such as the photobleaching and limited long-term stability [23]. Meanwhile, the pH-sensors based on organic dyes and nanocarriers still suffer from limited stability and weak biocompatibility due to the large size of organic dyes [24]. Thus, the prepared CQDs with ultra small size, brilliant photostability, good biocompatibility, safety, and unique pH-responsive property showed an excellent potential for pH sensing.

Furthermore, the prepared FBEP-CQDs exhibited an excellent potential for temperature sensing. Currently, nanomaterial-based thermal probes got great interest and wide applicability. In the past few years, various fluorescent nanomaterials such as semiconductor quantum dots, organic dyes, lanthanide complex, fluorescent polymers, metals nanoparticles, and inorganic and hybrid phosphors have been developed as thermal nanoprobes, which work via the fluorescence emission intensity. Nonetheless, these fluorescent nanomaterials have limited applications due to their high toxicity, poor stability, deprived accuracy, and poor sensitivity. Thus, there is a need for a non-toxic, sensitive, and inexpensive nanomaterial for thermal sensing. Despite the vast fields of applications of CQDs, limited work is devoted to their evaluation as nanothermal sensor [25–28]. Herein, the synthesized FBEP-CQDs exhibited excellent temperature-responsive fluorescence with high sensitivity over the range of 30–70 °C.

Overall, a multifunctional fluorescence nanosensor of FBEP-CQDs is developed based on a fast, facile, green, and economical one-pot microwave approach from the empty pods of fava beans as an abundant and cheap plant waste, which keeps prospect for assembly of a versatile sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
ce:section.ce:para: The instruments used in this study are presented in the supplementary material section “S1.1. Instruments”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and reagents
ce:section.ce:para: Analytical-grade solvents and chemicals were utilized. Sodium chloride, sodium citrate, sodium benzoate, glucose, barium chloride, magnesium chloride and calcium chloride were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., U.S.A. p-Nitrophenol (PNP, 99 %) was purchased from Wako Pure Chemical Industries, Japan. Sprite soft drink and Nestle and Glinter sparkling water were purchased from a local supermarket (Mansoura, Egypt). Human lung fibroblast (WI38) cell line was obtained from ATCC via Holding company for biological products and vaccines (VACSERA), Cairo, Egypt. Fresh green fava bean pods were purchased from a local grocery in Mansoura City, Dakahlia, Egypt.

Environmental water samples including river, sea, and tap water were obtained from Nile River branch (Mansoura City, Dakahlia, Egypt), Mediterranean Sea (Ras Elbar, Damietta, Egypt), and our laboratory tap, respectively. All samples were filtered with a 0.45 µm syringe filter and stored in the refrigerator (4 °C) till use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solutions and buffer
ce:section.ce:para: Britton Robinson buffer (BRB) was prepared from aqueous solutions of 0.04 M orthophosphoric acid, 0.04 M acetic acid, and 0.04 M boric acid then pH was adjusted using 0.2 M sodium hydroxide to pH 2.0–12.0. Moreover, a PNP stock solution (1.44 mM) was prepared in distilled water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of FBEP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The seeds of green fresh fava bean were removed from the pods then the empty pods were collected and thoroughly rinsed with distilled water to remove any debris matter. The empty pods were cut into small pieces and grounded with a domestic fruit mixer to obtain a homogenous matrix. The obtained matrix was passed through a gauze cloth to get rid of the coarse solid particles. Ten mL of the obtained liquid and 10 mL of distilled water were transferred to a small beaker and well-mixed. The obtained mixture was treated in a household microwave at 1700 W for 5 min, then left to cool down to room temperature. The obtained dark dry residue was scratched with a spatula and mixed with 20 mL distilled water, sonicated for 30 min, and filtered through Whatman filter paper. The obtained filtrate was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min. Finally, the supernatant was collected and filtered with 0.45 µm syringe filter. The obtained clear dark brown solution was kept in an amber-colored bottle in the refrigerator (4 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of fluorescence quantum yield of the FBEP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The relative quantum yield (Ф) of the synthesized FBEP-CQDs was calculated applying reported method [29] according to the procedure described in the supplementary material (Section S1.2.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for PNP determination
Construction of calibration curve for PNP
ce:section.ce:para: Accurate aliquots of 0.5 mL FBPP-CQDs, 0.5 mL of BRB (pH 10.0), and increasing volumes of PNP standard solution (1.44 mM) were transferred to a set of 10.0 mL volumetric flasks. The solutions were made up to the final volume using distilled water and the solutions were well-mixed. The fluorescence (F) was measured at 456 nm after excitation at 354 nm. The fluorescence (F0 ) of a blank experiment was recorded meanwhile. Every experiment has been conducted in triplicate. The calibration curve was constructed by plotting Ln (F0/F) against PNP concentration [μM] and the linear regression equation was calculated. All fluorescence measurements were carried out at room temperature (25 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of PNP in environmental water
ce:section.ce:para: In a group of 10 mL volumetric flasks, 5 mL of each water sample was mixed with 0.5 mL FBPP-CQDs, and 0.5 mL BRB. The solutions were completed to final volume with distilled water and well-mixed. The fluorescence (F) was measured at 456 nm after excitation at 354 nm with a 1.0 cm quartz cuvette. The fluorescence (F0 ) of a blank experiment was recorded meanwhile. All fluorescence measurements were carried out at room temperature (25 °C).

For spiking experiments, a series of 5.0 mL of each water sample was spiked with different concentrations of PNP (36.0, 72.0, and 144 µM) before proceeding as previously described. Blank experiment was done for each water sample. Three independent determinations were conducted for each concentration and the average values of (F) were utilized to calculate the %recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FBEP-CQDs as a pH sensor
Fluorescence-based determination of pH
ce:section.ce:para: In a group of 10 mL volumetric flasks, aliquots of BRB pH solution (pH 3.0–6.0 and pH 8.0–12.0, 1.0 mL) were mixed with CQDs working solution (1.0 mL), followed by the addition of distilled water to make up the final volume. The fluorescence spectra were recorded with a 1 cm quartz cuvette at 354/430 nm. Two linear calibration curves were constructed by plotting the pH value (3.0–6.0 and 8.0–12.0) versus the FI and the two regression equations were calculated. All pH measurements were carried out at room temperature (25 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application for determination of the pH of soft drinks by the developed pH nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of Sprite and Glinter soft drinks and Nestle sparkling water was determined using the synthesized CQDs. In a set of 10.0 mL volumetric flasks, 1.0 mL of FBEP-CQDs and 3.0 mL of each drink were added. The final volumes were adjusted to 10.0 mL using distilled water then well-mixed. Blank experiments were performed meanwhile by omitting the soft drink. Standard deviation and percent recovery were calculated. Besides, the pH of the three drinks was measured using a calibrated pH meter for comparison of the results. All pH measurements were carried out at room temperature (25 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: FBEP-CQDs as a nanothermometer
ce:section.ce:para: In a group of 10.0 mL volumetric flasks, 1.0 mL of FBEP-CQDs and 0.5 mL of BRB (pH 7.0) were added and the volume was completed with distilled water. The flasks were placed in a water bath and the temperature was adjusted to 30, 40, 50, 60, and 70 °C. The fluorescence intensity was recorded at each temperature at λex/λem of 354/430 nm. Each experiment was repeated three times. Blank experiments were performed for each temperature three times. Ln (F0/F) was plotted against temperature and the linear regression equation was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Microwave-assisted synthesis of waste-derived CQDs from fava beans empty pods
ce:section.ce:para: Fava bean is grown globally in cropping systems as one of the oldest plants. The edible part of fava bean is the seeds, while empty pods remain is a waste that constitures 70–75 % (w/w) of the fresh material of the crop yield. The empty pods of fava beans are composed mainly of crude fiber, hemicellulose, cellulose, lignin, crude protein, and starch. Fava bean pods contain considerable quantities of bioactive phytochemicals such as flavonoids and tannins, as well as significant amounts of elements like phosphorus and potassium [11]. These profuse components encouraged us to utilize the fava bean empty pods (FBEP) as a raw material for manufacturing CQDs.

Additionally, the applicability and reliability of the microwave-assisted synthesis method has been examined by utilizing fava beans pods collected from different regions in Egypt and at various cultivation seasons: Spring (May), Summer (August), and Autumn (September) as a feedstock. As demonstrated in Fig. 2 b, the fluorescence spectra and intensity are almost the same for all the tested alternates. These results show the applicability of the microwave-mediated method for the synthesis of CQDs in different laboratories at different times using available plant waste. Additionally, it has the privilege over many other synthesis methods as it is green, one-pot, rapid, safe, and cost-efficient.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical characterization of the prepared FBEP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The morphological appearance and size of the synthesized FBEP-CQDs were determined by TEM imaging. Fig. 3
a shows that the prepared CQDs have a spherical shape with even distribution. The particle size histogram (Fig. 3 b) shows that the average particle size of the FBEP-CQDs is 11.75 ± 2.24 nm. The XRD spectrum of the FBEP-CQDs was recorded in the range of 2θ = 0–80° (Fig. 3c). The main diffraction peak at 21.714 corresponds to (100) plane of carbon, approved the formation of amorphous graphitic carbon (sp2 hybridization). The interlayer spacing (d-value = 0.455 nm) is broader than that of graphite (0.34 nm). This is attributable to the surface oxygen containing groups probably have a role in increasing the interlayer space [30].

Moreover, FTIR spectroscopy was used to investigate the functional groups and the composition of the prepared CQDs as shown in Fig. 3 d. The IR spectrum shows a broad peak at 3331 cm−1 that is assigned to O–H or N–H stretching and the weak peak at 2200 cm−1 that is attributed to CC conjugated/CC/CN stretching. Eventually, the peak at 1636 cm−1 assigned to CN, CC (alkene or α,β-unsaturated ketone), CO stretching (ketone or amide), or N–H bending of amine. The peak at 583 cm−1 corresponds to C–S/C–S–C/S–S stretching [31]. Therefore, the FTIR spectrum displayed the presence of polar oxygen, sulfur, and nitrogen-containing functional groups which contributed to the hydrophilicity and high polarity of synthesized FBEP-CQDs. To corroborate this result, the surface charge of FBEP-CQDs was also determined by evaluating its Zeta potential (ζ) that was found to be −10.6 mV (Fig. 3 e). The negative charge on the surface of the CQDs results from the surface function groups such as hydroxyl group. The negative charges on the particles induce repulsive forces that impart stability to the synthesized CQDs.

The elemental analysis and surface composition of the manufactured FBEP-CQDs have been further confirmed by XPS analysis. The XPS results confirm that the FBEP-CQDs are composed mainly of atomic C (61.33 %), O (29.91 %), and N (5.11 %), in addition to some minor components including S (0.74 %), Cl (0.93 %), and Si (1.98 %). The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 3f) reveals C 1s at 286.09 eV, O 1s at 533.29 eV, N 1s at 400.93 eV, S 2p at 169.85 eV, Cl 2p at 198.89 eV, and Si 2p at 102.43 eV. As noted from Fig. S1a (Supplementary material), the deconvolution of C1s spectrum resulted in three carbon states of C–C/CC/C–H with a binding energy at 284.5 eV, C–N/C–OH/C-Cl at 286.14 eV, and CO/CN at 287.5 eV [32]. The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. S1b) proven the presence of pyrrolic N at 401.1 eV, pyridinic N/phenyl-NH2 at 399.58 eV [33], and CN at 400.54 eV [34]. In the O 1s high resolution spectrum (Fig. S1c), the presence of CO at 533.64 eV [35] and C–OH/C–O–C at 532.66 eV [33] has been confirmed. The S 2p high resolution spectrum (Fig. S1d) demonstrated three peaks of C–S–N at 162.66 eV, –C–SOx– (x = 2, 3, 4) species at 168.27 eV [36], and (C)2–S– at 166.41 eV [34]. Besides, the Cl 2p high resolution spectrum (Fig. S1e) indicates the presence of chlorobenzene at 200.08 eV and 201.9 eV. Meanwhile, the high-resolution spectrum of Si (Fig. S1f) showed two peaks at 102.46 and 102.33 eV for C-Si-Cl3 group [34].

Thus, the XPS and FTIR studies revealed that the FBEP-CQDs surface is functionalized with the above-mentioned functional groups which offer excellent polarity allowing monodispersity making them suited for nanosnsing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization and quantum yield of the prepared FBEP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Using a UV–VIS spectrophotometer, the optical characteristics of the synthesized FBEP-CQDs were investigated. FBEP-CQDs’ absorption spectra (Fig. S2a, Supplementary material) showed two bands: one at 210 nm, referring to π–π of the CC in the aromatic core of FBEP-CQDs, and another at 285 nm, which is attributed to the n–π* transition of the surface carbonyl/amine functional groups [37].

Furthermore, the fluorescence properties of the synthesized FBEP-CQDs have been investigated. Upon excitation at 354 nm, the maximum intense blue fluorescence was obtained at 430 nm (Fig. S2a, Supplementary material). However, applying λex over a range of 320–420 nm resulted in an obvious excitation-dependent emission (Fig. S2b, Supplementary material). The resulted spectra showed an increase in fluorescence intensity (FI) with increasing excitation wavelength from 320 to 340 nm with red shift of the emission wavelength. However, increasing the excitation wavelength from 350 to 420 nm led to a stepwise decrease in fluorescence intensity with continued red shift of the emission spectra. This excitation-dependent emission of the FBEP-CQDs is similar to that reported in literature [37,38] that is attributed to the particle size distribution of the CQDs and the abundance of surface –COOH and amine groups with the competition between plentiful emission spots [39]. The highest FI was obtained by applying λex/λem of 354/430 nm. The relative quantum yield (QY) of the prepared FBEP-CQDs was studied using quinine sulfate as a standard. The synthesized FBEP-CQDs from fava beans empty pods showed a remarkable merit over many other reported CQDs [6,40–52] as it has higher QY value of 16.9 % (Table S1, Supplementary Material).

Furthermore, the stability of the synthesized FBEP-CQDs solution was evident for at least 1 month without any instability feature as clustering, floatation, precipitation, or turbidity, where the change in the QY did not exceed 1.4 % after 1 month storage at 4 °C. Fig. S1c shows the fluorescence spectra of FBEP-CQDs obtained in different days for one month period indicating constant emission wavelength and intensity. Thus, the good stability of FBEP-CQDs qualified it for different multi-applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity MTT assay against WI-38 normal cell line
ce:section.ce:para:
The colorimetric MTT assay is based on the conversion of yellow tetrazolium bromide (MTT) in living cells into purple formazan derivatives by the mitochondrial succinate dehydrogenase enzyme. Normal WI-38 cell line (fetal lung fibroblast cells) was used to study the cytotoxicity of the synthesized CQDs in MTT assay. The reported procedure [53] was applied. The % relative cell viability was calculated using the following Eq.:
% Relative cell viability = (Absorbance of treated samples/Absorbance of untreated sample) × 100

Doxorubicin was used as a reference cytotoxic agent with IC50 of 2.60 ± 0.05 μg/mL to compare with the prepared CQDs [53]. As shown in Table 1
, the CQDs nearly have no cytotoxicity against normal WI-38 cell line with very high IC50 values (75.30 ± 3.8 μg/mL), compared to very small IC50 value of doxorubicin. Furthermore, at doses below 6.25 μg/mL, CQDs demonstrated 100 % cellular viability as shown in Table 1. Thus, the safety and non-toxicity of the prepared CQDs were confirmed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the proposed microwave-assisted synthesis of FBEP-CQDs and reported methods for synthesis of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The use of microwave radiation for CQDs synthesis speeds up the synthesis process, decreases the necessary time from many hours to a few minutes while providing a simple and straightforward synthesis platform with a distinct green character. Herein, we investigated fava bean empty pods as an alternate bio feedstock for the sustainable synthesis of CQD. The FBEP-CQDs represent an inventive step toward avoiding negative environmental impacts and implementing circular economy techniques, which are crucial to achieving sustainable development.

The proposed microwave-assisted synthesis of FBEP-CQDs proved its prevalence over the reported methods [6,40–52] revealed by its higher QY, increased greenness of the process by incorporating plant waste, dependence on conveniently accessible domestic microwave, cost-efficiency, combining different functional groups, excellent fluorescent properties and good stability of the produced CQDs. On the other hand, the reported synthesis methods [43–52] require harsh conditions as long heating time (2–24 h) and very high temperatures from 180 to 450 °C (Table S1, Supplementary material). The suggested procedure is 24–288 times quicker than the reported heat-induced hydrothermal methods. Additionally, vegetable waste was used as a carbon source without using any chemicals to synthesize the fluorescent CQDs. These features helped to increase the greenness of the synthetic procedure and to fully use plant parts and wastes that may be produced either from industries or domestic work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FBEP-CQDs as a turn-off fluorescence sensor for PNP
ce:section.ce:para:
Experimental study showed that PNP efficiently quenches the intense fluorescence of the FBEP-CQDs. Thus, the ability to use the synthesized FBEP-CQDs as a turn-off fluorescence sensor for the determination of PNP has been tested. After studying the absorbance of PNP and the fluorescence spectrum of the FBEP-CQDs, it was found that the UV spectrum of PNP showed overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of the CQDs (Fig. S2a, Supplementary material). This suggests the contribution of primary (pIFE) and secondary inner filter (sIFE) effects in the fluorescence quenching [54]. The addition of different concentrations of PNP (7.2–360.0 μM) leads to a stepwise quenching of the fluorescence of FBEP-CQDs (Fig. 4
a). In order to confirm the role of IFE in the fluorescence quenching and to verify whether it is the only quenching mechanism or there is other quenching mechanisms, the FI values have been corrected by removal of IFE to calculate the corrected fluorescence (Fcorrected ) using Eq. (2) [54]:
(2)
F
c o r r e c t e d
=
F
o b s e r v e d
× a n t i l o g (
A
ex
+
A
em
2
)
Fcorrected stands for FI after removal of IFE, Fobserved is the observed FI, and Aex and Aem denote for the PNP absorbance at the excitation and emission wavelengths of the FBEP-CQDs, respectively.

Afterwards, the % suppressed efficiency (%E) for the corrected and observed FI was calculated using Eq. (3) [54]]:
(3)
% E = [ 1 -
( F /
F 0
)
] × 100

Thus, the decline in %E upon subtracting the IFE confirmed the role of IFE (about 20 % of the %E was lost by exclusion of IFE) as shown in Fig. 4 b. This result confirmed the existence of another quenching mechanism that can be considered the main pillar for fluorescence quenching. Thus, Stern-Volmer analysis was performed at four increasing temperature settings (310, 320, 330 and 350 K) to identify whether dynamic or static quenching has a role in the fluorescence quenching. Dynamic quenching occurs due to the collision between quencher and fluorescent molecules in their excited state which is known to be enhanced with increasing the temperature. On the other hand, static quenching occurs when the quencher molecules form a non-fluorescent complex with the fluorescent molecules in their ground state. This type of quenching is decreased by decreasing the temperature [55]. Plots of
F
0
F
versus [PNP] have been constructed as shown in Fig. 4 c. according to Eq. (4) [56].
(4)
F 0
/ F = 1 + K s v
[ P N P ]
where Ksv = Stern-Volmer quenching constant and [PNP] = the molar concentration of PNP.

As illustrated in Table 2 , the Ksv increased by temperature increase which revealed the responsibility of dynamic mechanism for the fluorescence quenching. The collision of PNP molecules with the FBEP-CQDs resulted in energy transfer from the CQDs to PNP molecules through a radiation-less process ( Fig. 4 d). This is augmented by the repulsion between the negatively charged FBEP-CQDs and the negative PNP in the alkaline pH of the reaction medium. In conclusion, the results of the mechanism investigation show that the quenching of FBEP-CQDs was caused by a combination of IFE and dynamic quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical method optimization for PNP determination by the FBEP-CQDs nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: Different criteria were tested to identify the best conditions for PNP determination with the synthesized FBEP-CQDs fluorescence sensor. The influence of pH on the fluorescence quenching efficiency was investigated using BRB with pH ranges from 2.0 to 12.0. As shown in Fig. S3a (Supplementary material), the values of F0/F increased steadily with increasing the pH with a dramatic increase at pH 10.0, beyond which the value of F0/F remained constant. This behavior is attributed to bathochromic shift and hyperchromic effect of the UV-spectrum of PNP in alkaline medium induced by the formation of quinonoid structure and nitronate functionality. This lead to 18 times increase of PNP absorbance at 456 nm (λem of FBEP-CQDs) and 1.4 times increase of its absorbance at 357 nm (λex of CQDs) (Fig. S3b, Supplementary material). This effect is translated to a substantial improvement of the quenching of CQDs fluorescence by IFE. Besides, the negatively charged phenolate anion that occurs in the alkaline medium is expected to exert stronger repulsive action with the negative FBEP-CQDs which would significantly boost the dynamic collisional fluorescence quenching of FBEP-CQDs. Therefore, pH 10.0 was chosen as the optimum pH for the determination of PNP by FBEP-CQDs.

Moreover, the influence of the volume of FBEP-CQDs (0.25–1.0 mL) has been studied where it was found that 0.5 mL FBEP-CQDs was the optimum volume to achieve the best fluorescence quenching efficiency as illustrated in Fig. S3c (Supplementary material).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the analytical method for PNP determination
ce:section.ce:para:
The developed method’s performance was evaluated using the fluorescence spectra of the FBEP-CQDs in the presence of different PNP concentrations. A linear relation was obtained after plotting Ln (F0/F) values [57] versus different concentrations of PNP within the concentration range of 7.2–360.0 µM according to the regression equation:
(5)
L n
F 0
/ F
= 0.0119 x + 0.1306
r = 0.9999
where x denotes the PNP concentration [μM] and r is the correlation coefficient. The high sensitivity of the fluorescent sensor was confirmed by the values of limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) that were found to be 2.31 and 7.0 μM, respectively.

Additionally, accuracy, intra-, and inter-day precision of the developed method have been studied. Three PNP concentrations were tested three times on the same day and three days apart to evaluate both intra- and inter-day precision, respectively. The obtained small values of %RSD (≤2.413) and good values of %found indicate the high precision of the proposed method (Table 3 ). These results showed that the analysis using the prepared FBEP-CQDs fluorescence sensor obeys all validation criteria of the ICH guidelines [58] confirming its suitability as a fluorescence probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of FBEP-CQDs to different ions and co-existing species: Interference study
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence of the synthesized FBEP-CQDs in the presence of various species was evaluated by selectivity study. This study was performed in the presence of Na+, K+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Sr2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Li+, Cl-, I-, F-, CO3 2–, citrate, benzoate, glucose, sucrose, and sodium dodecyl sulfate. Referring to Table 4 , results showed no significant effect of any examined species on the FI of the FBEP-CQDs confirming the selectivity and applicability of FBEP-CQDs for environmental applications to detect PNP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real application of the FBEP-CQDs for determination of PNP in different surface water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Efficiency of the synthesized FBEP-CQDs were used for the detection and analysis of PNP in different real water samples of variable natures and sources. Nile River, Mediterranean Sea, and tap water samples were screened. The results showed the absence of PNP traces in the examined water samples. Thus, to ensure applicability of the method, water samples spiked with PNP were prepared. The FBEP-CQDs sensors were used for the analysis of spiked PNP samples yielding %recovery ranging from 100.12 to 101.01 % with standard deviation of ±1.06–2.8 as shown in Table 5 . These results confirmed the suitability of the developed nanosensor for PNP detection in complex samples such as environmental surface water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the FBEP-CQDs fluorescence sensor and the reported methods for PNP
ce:section.ce:para: The efficiency of some reported nanoparticle-based analytical techniques [14–21,57] was compared to that of the FBEP-CQDs fluorescence sensor for the determination of PNP as shown in Table S2 (Supplementary Material). The proposed method is less time and less energy consuming than the reported methods. Moreover, no hazardous chemicals were employed in the proposed method unlike other reported methods. These reported methods used some hazardous reagents and solvents for synthesis of the nanoparticle sensor such as CsBr, PbBr2, toluene, oleic acid, formaldehyde, pyridoxal, N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine, p-aminophenol, 2,4-diaminophenol hydrochlorideand N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)-propyl]-ethylenediamine. Additionally, the reported methods require high temperature up to 180 °C and long heating times for synthesizing the nanoparticles. On the contrary, the developed synthesis procedure takes 5 min utilizing a very cheap and accessible plant waste. Besides, the developed method is a mix-and-read approach since the inteaction is immediate between PNP and FBEP-CQDs, and no hazardous chemicals or harsh conditions were involved in the entire procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FBEP-CQDs as a pH-sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The prepared FBEP-CQDs exhibited a characteristic pH-responsive behavior at constant temperature (25 °C). The correlation between FI and pH was inspected by adding FBEP-CQDs to BRB over a wide pH range (3.0–12.0) as can be seen in Fig. 5
a. As presented in Fig. 5 b, significantly enhanced FL intensity was noted as the pH increased from 3.0 to 6.0, followed by a significant drop of FI from pH of 7.0–8.0. Next, the FI enhanced again over pH 8.0–12.0 ( Fig. 5 c). Interestingly, the emission peak showed a stepwise redshift at alkaline pH ( Fig. 5 c). The protonation state of FBEP-CQDs is very likely to affect the pH response. Based on the abundant amino and carboxylic groups on the surface of FBEP-CQDs, the deprotonation extent of FBEP-CQDs rises increasingly as the pH varies from acid to alkali. This is accompanied by the production of high level of deprotonated surface nitrogen and oxygen atoms, which alters the electron distribution [59]. Besides, this generates additional surface defects on FBEP-CQDs, and extends the length of conjugated chain, resulting in red-shifting of emission peak [24].

The correlation between pH and fluorescence intensity has been thoroughly studied. A good linearity was observed between FI and pH over the two linearity ranges ( Fig. 5 b &c insets): 3.0–6.0 and 8.0–12.0 according to the following regression equations:
FI = 83.4 p H + 225
R 2
= 0.9899
p H 3.0 - 6.0
FI = 38 p H + 251.8
R 2
= 0.9927
p H 8.0 - 12.0

Inspired by the effect of pH on FBEP-CQDs fluorescence intensity, the FBEP-CQDs were used as a pH sensor to determine the pH of various soft drinks. We examined the pH of two sparkling waters (Glinter and Nestle) and a soft drink (Sprite). The results listed in Table 6 were compared with the data obtained by using a commercial pH meter. The obtained results revealed a great agreement between the pH values determined by FBEP-CQDs and values obtained using pH meter as revealed by student t-test and variance ratio F-test wherer no significant difference was detected. This shows that the developed pH nanosensor is satisfactory for practical use.

The FBEP-CQDs pH-nanoprobe is superior to various nanomaterial-based pH sensors previously reported [60–64]. As shown in Table S3 (Supplementary Material), the linearity range of the new pH-nanoprobe is significantly wider than most of the reported methods. Besides, the new pH-nanoprobe is greener in nature than the reported nanoprobes that are mainly metal nanoparticles known for their toxicity and hazardous effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FBEP-CQDs as a nanothermometer
ce:section.ce:para: The FBEP-CQDs fluorescence response for the temperature (30–70 °C) was explored at a fixed pH (pH 7.0). As concluded from the results displayed in Fig. 5 d, the fluorescence intensity of FBEP-CQDs decreased proportionally with increasing temperature. In the temperature range of 30–70°C, the fluorescence intensity decreases by 24 % with increasing temperature. The fluorescence peak position of FBEP-CQDs exhibited very weak temperature dependence like some CQDs reported in the literature [65]. Generally, the process of the temperature-reliant character of CQDs is not well-understood. However, it is likely that the fluorescence decline with temperature can be ascribed to the temperature-brought energy traps on the CQDs surface, possibly prompting energy transmission and fluorescence quenching [66,67].

The linear regression of Ln (F0/F) versus temperature satisfies Ln (F0/F) = 0.0089 t − 0.239 (r = 0.9993), where F0 and F are the fluorescence of FBEP-CQDs before and after temperature elevation, respectively, and t is the temperature (°C). The obtained results manifest the feasibility of the new nanothermometer for temperature sensing.

The FBEP-CQDs nanothermometer surpasses many reported quantum dots-based nanothermometers [25,68–70] as revealed from the data in Table S4 (Supplementary Material). The developed nanothermometer is much greener than reported nanothermometers that are derived from either chemical carbon sources or metals and need harsh synthesis conditions. Meanwhile, the new nanothermometer exhibited excellent temperature linearity range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green chemistry and circular economy aspects
ce:section.ce:para: Green chemistry is essential for managing environmental pollution levels and resolving global environmental issues. As a result, the development and evaluation of green analytical procedures based on the use of renewable green reagents, the elimination of dangerous compounds, and the consumption of minimal energy become essential. From this point, different matrices were applied to assess the greenness of analytical methods involving complementary green analytical procedure index (ComplexGAPI) [71] and Analytical GREEnness calculator (AGREE) [72] approaches.

ComplexGAPI software [71] is specifically designed for evaluating the greenness property of the analytical method that use materials synthesized before the analytical step, such as FBEP-CQDs in the developed method. As shown in Fig. S4a, the upper pentagons represent the green qualities of the analytical technique involving reagents, procedures, and equipment, recognizing many factors such as quantity, health effect, reagent and solvent safety, energy consumption, waste generation, and method nature. Additionally, the hexagon in the bottom refers to the level of greenness of the synthesis step. It involves the evaluation of the conditions, safety and health hazards, purification, and work up. The green, yellow, and red colors refer to low, medium, and high environmental risks. The obtained pictogram (Fig. S4a) showed that both procedures used in the synthesis of FBEP-CQDs, and the analysis step satisfies many green criteria.

Furthermore, AGREE approach [72] was applies to evaluate the greenness of the proposed method. This approach quantifies the greenness of the designed procedure by evaluating the quantities, toxicity, released waste, spent energy, and the number of steps used. The resulted score (Fig. S4b) is 0.78 which confirms the greenness of the developed method.

The results of greenness evaluation tools corroborate the greenness of the developed technique. The synthesis of FBEP-CQDs was readily made at mild settings that are safe for both humans and the environment. When compared to other hydrothermal methods, the new approach is less expensive, less energy-intensive, and takes less time to complete. Furthermore, it relies on conversion of a low-value plant waste into a value-added FBEP-CQDs with multiple sensing applications, which boosts its eco-friendliness by avoiding toxic chemicals and solvents. The suggested approach has numerous green characteristics that aid in bringing the sustainability concept to analytical chemistry laboratories. The developed approach is a circular economy model by extending the life cycle of materials by recycling and reusing of plant waste like fava beans empty pods and creating a further value via FBEP-CQDs synthesis. This, in turn, protects the environment, minimizing raw material need, and saves money. This qualifies the developed approach for widespread use in low-income laboratories.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In the current study, a novel and one-pot efficient method was applied for the synthesis of FBEP-CQDs. The pods of fava beans were used for the first time as a cheap, available, green, and renewable substrate for the synthesis of FBEP-CQDs. The microwave-based synthesis approach is quick, one-pot, simple, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly, with the synthesis achieved at 1700 W in 5 min without the need of any chemicals or solvents. The produced FBEP-CQDs were thoroughly examined utilizing TEM, XPS, XRD, FTIR, Zeta potential measurement, fluorescence, UV–VIS spectroscopy. Furthermore, the synthesized FBEP-CQDs displayed a high fluorescence quantum yield of 16.9 % as well as water solubility and stability. These results nominated the multifaceted application of the synthesized FBEP-CQDs as a multipurpose sensor. The FBEP-CQDs sensor has been applied for the determination of PNP within concentration range of 7.2–360.0 μM in river, tap, and sea water. Moreover, the synthesized FBEP-CQDs were used as a pH nanoprobe, where it showed linearity within pH ranges of 3.0–6.0 and 8.0–12.0. Additionally, it was used as a nanothermometer within temperature range of 30–70 °C. Ultimately, the new analytical approach demonstrated exceptional greenness because it is based on a plant waste product. Furthermore, the proposed process is free of toxic chemicals, organic solvents, high temperatures, and time consumption. Importantly, the created method is regarded as a step forward for the implementation of circular economy practices in research laboratories, making it ideal for institutes with minimal resources.
